text.title: GitHub Projects Structure and Configuration
text.summary: Project types, custom field setup, view configuration, and milestone integration for GitHub Projects v2.

rule.statement: Use GitHub Projects v2 (not classic) for all new projects; use organization-level projects for multi-repo work.
rule.rationale: Projects v2 supports custom fields, iterations, cross-repo items, and table/board/roadmap views. Classic projects are limited to simple kanban with no custom fields.

example.note: Organization project: pulls issues from repo-A and repo-B into one board; each item carries a Repository field for filtering.

section.title: Custom Fields

rule.statement: Define these standard custom fields at project creation: Status (single_select), Priority (P0–P3), Estimate (number), Sprint (iteration, 2-week duration), Team (single_select), Release (single_select).
rule.rationale: Fields defined upfront give agents a stable schema; adding fields mid-project requires backfilling existing items.

section.reference: Project custom fields and views configuration

rule.statement: Create three standard views: Table (all issues, sort by Priority), Board (sprint filter, group by Status), Roadmap (group by Release, month zoom).
rule.rationale: Table for triage, Board for sprint execution, Roadmap for stakeholder communication — three views cover 90% of PM needs without proliferating custom views.

example.note: Board view filter: sprint:@current — shows only current iteration items; WIP limits set per Status column.

rule.statement: Use milestones for release-scoped work; use iteration fields for sprint-scoped work — do not conflate them.
rule.rationale: Milestones track what ships in a release (v1.0, v1.1); iterations track what the team works on in a sprint. Mixing them makes both metrics inaccurate.

rule.statement: Link PRs to issues with "Fixes #123" in the PR description to trigger auto-close and built-in workflow progression.
rule.rationale: Auto-close on merge fires the built-in "Item closed → Done" workflow without agent or manual intervention.
